items.title: Heat waves mess with your brain. Scientists are trying to figure out why.
items.content: It’s been hot in London this week. Really hot. A dangerous heat wave has hit Western Europe. Yesterday, the UK recorded its highest ever June temperature at 36.1 °C (about 97 °F). But as the weather app on my phone confirmed, it felt like 39 °C. It’s frightening that we are seeing such temperatures in…

US Stock Market: Warsh faces crucial week with court ruling, global debut in focus
items.content: Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh faces a crucial week. The Supreme Court's decision on Governor Lisa Cook's removal will test the central bank's independence. Simultaneously, Warsh will present his new communication strategy, shifting away from explicit forward guidance, at a major international forum in Portugal. This approach, emphasizing data-driven decisions, contrasts with other central banks and carries global implications due to the dollar's dominance.

items.title: Hexaware Technologies shares jump 8% after securing Anthropic authorised reseller status for Amazon Bedrock
items.content: Hexaware Technologies shares rose sharply on Monday after the IT services company became an Anthropic Authorised Reseller for Amazon Bedrock. The designation allows Hexaware to sell, integrate and support Claude AI models for enterprises, strengthening its AI offerings and expanding its enterprise-focused generative AI capabilities across multiple industries.

Astronomers found two rare super puff planets lighter than cotton candy
items.content: Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight atmospheres could provide valuable clues about how some of the strangest planets in the galaxy come to exist.
items.summary: Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight a...

items.title: Einstein Probe may have caught a black hole tearing apart a white dwarf for the first time
items.content: Astronomers may have witnessed one of the rarest and most dramatic cosmic events ever seen: a long-sought intermediate-mass black hole ripping apart a dense white dwarf star and devouring it. The Einstein Probe space telescope caught the explosion in its earliest moments, revealing an unusual sequence of intense X-ray flashes unlike anything seen in a typical gamma-ray burst.
